narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the effort you've put into your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 31, upper-middle family income, and full-time student status, along with your rankings in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 91.7% chance that you would fail it—far higher than what it sees in most applicants who get approved. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger signals of bar exam success. The two biggest factors pushing the model toward this failure prediction were your low grade decile in your third year, which had the strongest negative influence, and your similarly low decile from your first year, which also significantly weighed against you compared to typical students who rank around the middle of their classes. Your family income level played a moderate role in the same direction, suggesting the model sees it as less favorable than average, while your LSAT score had a very minor push toward failure. On a more positive note, planning to attend full-time nudged things slightly toward success, aligning with the vast majority of students, and your undergraduate GPA had a tiny favorable effect, though both were much less influential overall. Compared to typical applicants—who have higher average GPAs around 3.21, LSAT scores near 37, and mid-level grade deciles—your early and later academic rankings stood out as the key mismatches driving the decision. This doesn't define your potential, and many students in similar situations have improved through targeted efforts. We recommend retaking courses or seeking academic coaching to boost your grade deciles, preparing more intensively for the LSAT to aim above the average, and perhaps exploring part-time options or gap-year programs that have helped others strengthen their profiles. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing forward, and consider reapplying after addressing these areas. We're rooting for you.
